perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall endeavor to be responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall be legally responsible" with "shall endeavor to be responsible" weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. Endeavoring does not guarantee actual responsibility, creating a loophole.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code Section 1439 - Performance of conditions; necessity
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1439 states that before any party to an obligation can require another party to perform any act which the first party should have performed, it is necessary to offer to perform it oneself, and the other party must be able to perform. By changing "shall be" to "endeavor to be" the buyer's responsibility becomes vague and less enforceable, potentially violating the spirit and requirement of California law that mandates clear performance of obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1439. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. INTERPRETATION OF OBLIGATIONS [1429 - 1451] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Conditional Obligations [1434 - 1442] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1439. Before any party to an obligation can require another party to perform any act under it, he must fulfill all conditions precedent thereto imposed upon himself; and must be able and offer to fulfill all conditions concurrent so imposed upon him on the like fulfillment by the other party, except as provided by the next section. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "shall be legally responsible" with "shall endeavor to be responsible," which weakens the legal obligation. California Civil Code Section 1439 states that before a party can require another to perform, that party must be able to perform, implying a clear and enforceable obligation. By using "endeavor to be", the buyer's responsibility becomes vague and less enforceable. This contradicts the legal expectation for clear performance of obligations as it introduces ambiguity, creating a loophole, and it is less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees not to disclose Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have generally agreed to be bound by the terms of this agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Changing "have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement" to "have generally agreed" removes the explicit requirement for a signed agreement, making it unclear how the advisors and affiliates are actually bound, creating a loophole regarding proof of agreement to confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets. It implicitly requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By weakening the contractual requirement for advisors and affiliates to *sign* an agreement, the "reasonable efforts" to protect confidential information are diminished, potentially violating CUTSA if a trade secret is misappropriated due to this weakened obligation. 'Generally agreed' is not sufficient evidence of agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the agreement from 'agreed by signing a copy of this agreement' to 'generally agreed'. The Law Explanation states that the CUTSA protects trade secrets and implicitly requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and that 'generally agreed' is not sufficient evidence of agreement. Snippet 1 cites California Code, CIV 3426 which states 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.' By removing the explicit requirement of a signed agreement, the changed text weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to protect confidential information, which the Law Explanation says potentially violates CUTSA. This falls under the definition of Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation because by making the agreement ambiguous, it may result in non-compliance with regulatory requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer will not rely on the information provided by Broker or Seller , including the Confidential Information , but shall conduct its own independen t due diligence .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer will consider the information provided by Broker or Seller , including the Confidential Information , and may conduct its own independent due diligence .
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will not rely" with "will consider" and "shall conduct" with "may conduct" transforms a mandatory requirement for independent due diligence into an optional one, creating a significant ambiguity. It is now unclear if the Buyer *must* perform due diligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 25401 prohibits any person from offering or selling a security by means of any written or oral communication that includes an untrue statement of a material fact or omits to state a material fact necessary to make the statements made, in light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. It creates a duty for accurate and full disclosure. While this section primarily applies to securities transactions, the underlying principle extends to business sales where material misrepresentations can occur. Removing the *requirement* for due diligence increases the risk that a buyer will rely on potentially misleading information. Though the seller provides the data, the language in the original text suggests due diligence must be undertaken, which protects both the buyer and seller. By weakening this clause, it increases the odds of a future breach of Section 25401.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 25401. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 4. SECURITIES [25000 - 31528] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 1949, Ch. 384. ) DIVISION 1. CORPORATE SECURITIES LAW OF 1968 [25000 - 25707] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1968, Ch. 88. ) PART 5. FRAUDULENT AND PROHIBITED PRACTICES [25400 - 25404] ( Part 5 added by Stats. 1968, Ch. 88. ) 25401. It is unlawful for any person to offer or sell a security in this state, or to buy or offer to buy a security in this state, by means of any written or oral communication that includes an untrue statement of a material fact or omits to state a material fact necessary to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which the statements were made, not misleading. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 190, Sec. 19. (AB 1517) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the buyer's obligation to conduct due diligence, potentially increasing the risk of the buyer relying on misleading information, which could violate California Corporations Code Section 25401. The original text mandated independent due diligence, whereas the changed text makes it optional. The scraped snippet from CORP 25401 states, 'It is unlawful for any person to offer or sell a security in this state...by means of any written or oral communication that includes an untrue statement of a material fact or omits to state a material fact necessary to make the statements made...not misleading.' By making due diligence optional, the changed text increases the risk of a buyer acting on incomplete or misleading information.
